revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:أمه نو أوزومه]][[as:আমে-ন′-উজুমে]][[bcl:Ama-no-Uzume]][[ca:Ama-no-Uzume]][[cs:Ama no Uzume]][[de:Amenouzume]][[el:Άμε Νο Ουζούμε]][[en:Ame-no-Uzume]][[eo:Ame no Uzume]][[es:Ame-no-Uzume]][[fa:آمه نو اوزومه]][[fr:Uzume]][[hr:Ame-no-Uzume]][[hu:Ame-no-Uzume]][[id:Ama-no-Uzume]][[it:Ama-no-Uzume]][[ja:アメノウズメ]][[jv:Ama-no-Uzume]][[ka:ამე-ნო უძუმე]][[ko:아메노우즈메]][[mni:ꯑꯃꯦ-ꯅꯣ-ꯎꯓꯨꯃꯦ]][[no:Ama-no-Uzume]][[pl:Amenouzume]][[pt:Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]][[ru:Амэ-но удзумэ]][[simple:Ama-no-Uzume]][[sv:Uzume]][[th:อาเมะ โนะ อูซูเมะ]][[tr:Ame-no-Uzume]][[uk:Ама-но-Удзуме-но-Мікото]][[zh:天鈿女命]]
{{Short description|Goddess of dawn, mirth and revelry in the Shinto religion of Japan|qq=}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | image = Uzume.jpg | caption = The statue of Ame-no-Uzume at [[Amanoiwato Shrine|Amanoiwato-jinja]] | name = Ame-no-Uzume | god_of = Goddess of the Dawn, meditation, and the arts | consort = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] | Greek_equivalent = {{ill|Eos|en|Eos|qid=Q178417|lt=Eos|1=Eos}}<ref name="witzel"/> | Roman_equivalent = {{ill|Aurora (mythology)|en|Aurora (mythology)|qid=Q178831|lt=Aurora|1=Aurora (mythology)}}<ref name="witzel"/> | Hinduism_equivalent = {{ill|Ushas|en|Ushas|qid=Q579072|lt=Ushas|1=Ushas}}<ref name="witzel"/> | equivalent1_type = Nuristani | equivalent1 = {{ill|Disani|en|Disani|qid=Q111915785|lt=Disani|1=Disani}}<ref name="witzel"/> }} {{Shinto|qq=}} {{Nihongo|'''Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto'''|, |lead=yes|qq=}} is the {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|qid=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} of [[:en:Dawn goddess|dawn]], mirth, meditation, revelry and the arts in the [[Shinto]] religion of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, and the wife of fellow-god [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]. (-no-Mikoto is a common honorific appended to the names of Japanese gods; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'.) She famously helped draw out the missing sun deity, [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], when she had hidden herself in a cave. Her name can also be pronounced as '''Ama-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto'''. She is also known as Ōmiya-no-Me-no-Ōkami, an ''{{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} [[kami]]'' possibly due to her relationship with her husband.<ref>{{cite web|title=Treasures of the Morikami|url=http://www.morikami.org/exhibits/treasures-of-the-morikami/|publisher=Morikami Museum and Japanese Gardens|access-date=23 August 2012|archive-date=21 July 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160721221437/http://morikami.org/exhibits/treasures-of-the-morikami/|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> She is also known as Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto, The Great Persuader, and The Heavenly Alarming Female.<ref>{{cite book|last=Herbert|first=Jean|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=K61O6sinRHcC&pg=PA264|title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan|publisher=Taylor & Francis|year=20dd|isbn=978-0-203-84216-4|pages=264|qq=}}</ref> She is depicted in [[kyōgen]] farce as Okame, a woman who revels in her sensuality.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012|qq=}} == Mythology == === Amaterasu and the cave === Amaterasu's brother, the storm god [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susano'o]], had vandalized her rice fields, threw a flayed horse at her loom, and brutally killed one of her maidens due to a quarrel between them. In turn, Amaterasu became furious with him and retreated into the Heavenly Rock Cave, [[Amano-Iwato]]. The world, without the illumination of the sun, became dark and the gods could not lure Amaterasu out of her hiding place. [[File:Uzume dancing.jpg|left|thumb|Uzume dancing to lure out Amaterasu.]] The clever Uzume overturned a tub near the cave entrance and began to dance on it, tearing off her clothing in front of the other deities. They considered this so comical that they laughed heartily at the sight.<ref name=":0">{{cite book|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA27|title=Japan Encyclopedia|publisher=Harvard University Press|year=2002|isbn=0-674-00770-0|pages=27–28|qq=}}</ref> This dance is said to have founded the Japanese ritual dance, [[Kagura]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Lancashire|first=Terence|year=2004|title=From Spirit Possession to Ritual Theatre: A Potential Scenario for the Development of Japanese [[Kagura]]|journal=Yearbook for Traditional Music|publisher=International Council for Traditional Music|volume=36|pages=90–108|doi=10.1017/S0740155800020476 |issn=0740-1558|jstor=20058793|qq=}}</ref> Further, this is also supported by other traditions claiming that the first kagura was danced by a shamaness who was a kami herself, Ame-no-Uzume, through luring (invoking the presence of) Amaterasu, thereby reenacting the intentions of the kagura as an act of communication to other deities.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sadler |first1=Albert |title="O-Kagura". Field Notes on the Festival Drama in Modern Tokyo |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |date=1970 |volume=29 |pages=275–300 |doi=10.2307/dd77614 |jstor=dd77614 |url=https://doi.org/10.2307/dd77614 |access-date=17 November 2023|qq=}}</ref> Moreover, in addition to inspiring the origins of the kagura, the myth of Ame-no-Uzume has also influenced the sources of three Shamanic elements later being incorporated into Shinto ritual and the kagura, being the Omoto Kagura, Hana Matsuri, and the Hayachine Kagura in presenting shamanic choreography as ritual communication, what the myth is most known for contributing.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Averbuch |first1=Irit |title=Shamanic Dance in Japan: The Choreography of Possession in Kagura Performance |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |date=1998 |volume=57 |page=293 |doi=10.2307/dd77614 |jstor=dd77614 |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/224531474 |access-date=17 November 2023|id={{ProQuest|224531474|qq=}} |qq=}}</ref> Uzume had hung a bronze mirror and a beautiful jewel of polished jade. Amaterasu heard them, and peered out to see what the commotion was about. When she opened the cave, she saw the jewel and her glorious reflection in a mirror which Uzume had placed on a tree, and slowly came out from her clever hiding spot. At that moment, the god {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|en|Ame-no-Tajikarao|qid=Q849412|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarawo-no-mikoto|1=Ame-no-Tajikarao}} dashed forth and closed the cave behind her, refusing to budge so that she could no longer retreat. Another god tied a magic {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} (compare the {{ill|Nuristanis|en|Nuristanis|qid=Q737671|lt=Nuristani|1=Nuristanis}} myth of a chaff attaching itself to the thread around a house near heaven) across the entrance.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=I6iwdB4oi7IC&pg=PA17|title=Traditional Japanese Arts And Culture: An Illustrated Sourcebook|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|year=2006|isbn=978-0-8248-2878-3|editor1=Addiss, Stephen|pages=16–17|editor2=Groemer, Gerald|editor3=Rimer, Thomas|qq=}}</ref> The deities [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame-no-Koyane-no-mikoto]] and [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-mikoto]] then asked Amaterasu to rejoin the divine. She agreed, and light was restored to the earth. === Uzume and Sarutahiko === [[File:Saruta-hiko and Uzume illustration by William Aston.jpg|thumb|left]] Amaterasu orders Uzume to accompany her grandson [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on his journey to earth. They head to ''[[Ame-no-ukihashi]]'' ("floating bridge of heaven") so they could head to earth but they are blocked by [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. Uzume comes and persuades Sarutahiko to let Ninigi pass, in other versions of the story Uzume flirts with Sarutahiko. During this journey, she cut the mouth of the sea cucumber with her dagger, causing it to fall permanently silent.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2008 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533262-9 |series=Handbooks of world mythology |location=Oxford |pages=dd1–dd2|qq=}}</ref> Later, Uzume and Sarutahiko fall in love and got married. Together, they found the {{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en|qq=}} </ref> == Worship == [[File:AmanoiwatoTablet.jpg|thumb|Tablet at the Ama-no-Uzume shrine in {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|qid=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}}|left|254x254px]] Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto is still worshiped today as a [[Shinto]] [[kami]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Tsubaki Sukeikai|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/contact/TsubakiKoApplication2.pdf|access-date=23 August 2012|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|qq=}}{{dead link|date=December 2017|bot=InternetArchiveBot|fix-attempted=yes|qq=}}</ref> There are many shrines dedicated to the goddess including Chiyo shrine, {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|qid=Q840967|lt=Tsubaki America Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America}} and {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|qid=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|title=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|website=www.tsubakishrine.org|access-date=Nov 23, 2020|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://visit.hikoneshi.com/en/plan_your_visit/sightseeing/temples-shrines/chiyo-shrine/|title=Chiyo Shrine |website=Hikone Travel Guide |date=5 September 2017|access-date=Nov 23, 2020|qq=}}</ref> In some, she is worshiped as a patroness of dancers and performers.<ref name=":1" /> Furthermore, there are connections between the myths of Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto alongside the eighth-century mythic texts that describe the similar yet unique mythology between 21st-century Japan and modern myth scholarship; being that there are debates both in the Japanese and English languages in terms of rendering the names of specific places, objects and characters in the Kiki to modern script. Moreover, the debate comes down to some characters in the Kiki having their Chinese characters within their name being consistent in translation while others are not, an example being with Ame-no-Uzume in different texts using different orthographies. As such, it is theorized that this decision is significant to the names in referring to the character phonetically as an identity that is of multiple sources, with scholars writing Ame-no-Uzume's name in katakana heavily implying that the ideal form of the figure is within an abstract dimension rather than limited to a single textual appearance, asserting her outside of specific historically-grounded texts.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Sheets |first1=William |title=Mythology in 21st Century Japan: A Study of Ame no Uzume no Mikoto |url=http://rave.ohiolink.edu/etdc/view?acc_num=osu1500615882214031 |website=Electronic Theses & Dissertations Center |publisher=Ohio State University |access-date=17 November 2023|qq=}}</ref> Along with her husband, she is addressed during the November festival of Tori-no-ichi in which masks or pictures of the pair are sold.<ref name=":1" /> ==Similarities to Vedic religion== According to {{ill|Michael Witzel|en|Michael Witzel|qid=Q78324|lt=Michael Witzel|1=Michael Witzel}}, Uzume is most closely related to the {{ill|Historical Vedic religion|en|Historical Vedic religion|qid=Q194497|lt=Vedic|1=Historical Vedic religion}} goddess {{ill|Ushas|en|Ushas|qid=Q579072|lt=Ushas|1=Ushas}} (''uṣás''), a descendant of the {{ill|Proto-Indo-European mythology|en|Proto-Indo-European mythology|qid=Q1401124|lt=Proto-Indo-European|1=Proto-Indo-European mythology}} goddess [[:en:Hausos|Hausos]] (''{{PIE|*h₂éwsōs|qq=}}''), and the {{ill|Nuristanis|en|Nuristanis|qid=Q737671|lt=Nuristani|1=Nuristanis}} goddess {{ill|Disani|en|Disani|qid=Q111915785|lt=Disani|1=Disani}}. Both goddesses share many similarities such as the cave ({{ill|Vala (Vedic)|en|Vala (Vedic)|qid=Q4102693|lt=Vala|1=Vala (Vedic)}}/[[Amano-Iwato|Iwato]]) and the exposure of breasts as a sign of friendship. Witzel proposed that the Japanese, Vedic, and Nuristani religions are much more closely related compared to other mythologies under what he calls Laurasian mythology, and that the three myths, may go back to the Indo-Iranian period, around 2000 BCE. This would make Uzume analogous to the {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|qid=Q34726|lt=Greek goddess|1=Greek mythology}} {{ill|Eos|en|Eos|qid=Q178417|lt=Eos|1=Eos}} and the {{ill|Roman mythology|en|Roman mythology|qid=Q122173|lt=Roman goddess|1=Roman mythology}} {{ill|Aurora (mythology)|en|Aurora (mythology)|qid=Q178831|lt=Aurora|1=Aurora (mythology)}}.<ref name="witzel">{{cite book|last=Witzel|first=Michael|title=Vala and Iwato: The Myth of the Hidden Sun in India, Japan, and beyond|year=2005|url=https://www.onmarkproductions.com/monkey-mythology-compare-india-japan-by-michael-witzel.pdf|qq=}}</ref> == References == {{reflist|qq=}} *{{cite book |title= Mythology: The Illustrated Anthology of World Myth and Storytelling |last= Littleton |first= C. Scott |author-link= C. Scott Littleton |year= 2002 |publisher= Duncan Baird Publishers |location= London |pages= 464–467|qq=}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto|qq=}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20120716205457/http://www.uwec.edu/philrel/shimbutsudo/uzume.html A substantial article on this subject] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20071223085703/http://www.goddessgift.com/goddess-myths/japanese_goddess_amaterasu.htm Amaterasu and Uzume, Goddesses of Japan, at Goddess Gift] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20041206223721/http://www.nami.it/Glos/AmenoUzume.htm A one-paragraph glossary entry in Italian] {{jmyth navbox long|qq=}} {{Sarutahiko Faith|qq=}}
[[wiktionary:天宇受売命|天宇受売命]] [[wiktionary:天鈿女命|天鈿女命]]
[[en:Ame-no-Uzume]]
